([Jawi](/wiki/Jawi_script "Jawi script"): ايستان نور الإيمان; [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"): The Light of Faith Palace) is the [official residence](/wiki/Official_residence "Official residence") of the [Sultan of Brunei](/wiki/Sultan_of_Brunei "Sultan of Brunei"), [Hassanal Bolkiah](/wiki/Hassanal_Bolkiah "Hassanal Bolkiah"), and the seat of the [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") government. The palace is located on a leafy, riverside sprawl of hills on the banks of the [Brunei River](/wiki/Brunei_River "Brunei River"), a few kilometres southwest of [Bandar Seri Begawan](/wiki/Bandar_Seri_Begawan "Bandar Seri Begawan"), Brunei's capital city. The palace is often at the centre of state occasions and royal hospitality.

Ploysri Porananond; Victor T. King (23 September 2016). [*Tourism and Monarchy in Southeast Asia*](https://books.google.com/books?id=rfJTDgAAQBAJ&pg=PA28). Cambridge Scholars Publishing. pp. 28–29. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1-4438-1661-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-4438-1661-8 "Special:BookSources/978-1-4438-1661-8").

["Largest residential palace"](https://web.archive.org/web/20150530064553/http://www.guinnessworldrecords.com/world-records/largest-residential-palace). [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records"). Archived from [the original](http://www.guinnessworldrecords.com/world-records/largest-residential-palace) on 30 May 2015. Retrieved 30 May 2015.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Official residence of the Sultan of Brunei

| Istana Nurul Iman | |
| --- | --- |
| Aerial photograph of the palace in 2024 | |
| Map Interactive map of the Istana Nurul Iman area | |
| General information | |
| Architectural style | [Brunei's Islamic and Malay](/wiki/Malay_Islamic_Monarchy "Malay Islamic Monarchy") |
| Location | [Bandar Seri Begawan](/wiki/Bandar_Seri_Begawan "Bandar Seri Begawan"), [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") |
| Completed | 1984 |
| Cost | US$3 Billion (BN$4 Billion) |
| Technical details | |
| Structural system | Malay and Islam |
| Floor count | Approximate 17 include underground floors |

| Design and construction | |
| Architects | [Leandro Locsin](/wiki/Leandro_Locsin "Leandro Locsin") (overall) and [Khuan Chew](/w/index.php?title=Khuan_Chew&action=edit&redlink=1 "Khuan Chew (page does not exist)") (interior)[[1]](#cite_note-PorananondKing2016-1) |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Istana_Nurul_Iman.jpg/250px-Istana_Nurul_Iman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Istana_Nurul_Iman.jpg)

The banquet hall, which can seat up to 5,000 people

The **Istana Nurul Iman** ([Jawi](/wiki/Jawi_script "Jawi script"): ايستان نور الإيمان; [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"): The Light of Faith Palace) is the [official residence](/wiki/Official_residence "Official residence") of the [Sultan of Brunei](/wiki/Sultan_of_Brunei "Sultan of Brunei"), [Hassanal Bolkiah](/wiki/Hassanal_Bolkiah "Hassanal Bolkiah"), and the seat of the [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") government. The palace is located on a leafy, riverside sprawl of hills on the banks of the [Brunei River](/wiki/Brunei_River "Brunei River"), a few kilometres southwest of [Bandar Seri Begawan](/wiki/Bandar_Seri_Begawan "Bandar Seri Begawan"), Brunei's capital city. The palace is often at the centre of state occasions and royal hospitality.

## Design and construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Istana_Nurul_Iman&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Design and construction")]

The name "Istana Nurul Iman" is taken from Malay *Istana* and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") *Nur-ul Imaan* and means *Palace of the Light of Faith.* It was designed by Filipino Visayan architect [Leandro V. Locsin](/wiki/Leandro_V._Locsin "Leandro V. Locsin"), who utilized the architectural motif of golden domes and vaulted roofs to echo Brunei's Islamic and Malay influences. The interior of the palace was designed by Dale Keller Interior designers. Construction was handled by Ayala Abbott and Butters, a UK construction firm and shop fitters, Hagen International from Denmark.

The palace had 10,000 people working on it from 17 countries across the globe.

It was completed in 1984 at a total cost of around [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")1.4 billion.

## World's largest palace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Istana_Nurul_Iman&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: World's largest palace")]

Main article: [World's largest palace](/wiki/World%27s_largest_palace "World's largest palace")

The title of the "world's largest palace" is difficult to ascertain, and controversial, as different countries use their own unique standard to claim that their palace is the largest. Using various definitions, a number of palaces are claimed to be the world's largest: Istana Nurul Iman, [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace"), [Quirinal Palace](/wiki/Quirinal_Palace "Quirinal Palace"), [Abdeen Palace](/wiki/Abdeen_Palace "Abdeen Palace"), the [Royal Palace of Madrid](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Madrid "Royal Palace of Madrid"), [Stockholm Palace](/wiki/Stockholm_Palace "Stockholm Palace"), the [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City"), the [Palace of Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles"), the [Royal Palace of Caserta](/wiki/Palace_of_Caserta "Palace of Caserta"), the [Winter Palace](/wiki/Winter_Palace "Winter Palace"), the [Louvre Palace](/wiki/Louvre_Palace "Louvre Palace"), [Prague Castle](/wiki/Prague_Castle "Prague Castle"), the [Grand Palace](/wiki/Grand_Palace "Grand Palace") and the [Palace of the Parliament](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Parliament "Palace of the Parliament").

To be considered for the Guinness World Record, the palace must have been intended for use as a royal residence, and only the combined area of the palace's indoor floors (a measurement commonly known as floorspace) is considered. As measured by these standards, Guinness World Records currently considers Istana Nurul Iman to hold the title as the "world's largest residential palace."[[2]](#cite_note-2)
Upon completion, Istana Nurul Iman became the largest residential palace in the world and the largest single-family residence ever built.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The palace contains 1,788 rooms, which includes 257 bathrooms, a banquet hall that can be expanded to accommodate up to 5,000 guests, and a mosque accommodating 1,500 people.[[4]](#cite_note-Bartholomew-4) The palace also includes a 110-car garage, an air conditioned stable for the Sultan's 200 polo ponies, and five swimming pools. In total, Istana Nurul Iman contains 2,152,782 square feet (200,000 m2) of floorspace.[[4]](#cite_note-Bartholomew-4) Istana Nurul Iman has 564 chandeliers, 51,000 light bulbs, 44 stairwells and 18 elevators.[[4]](#cite_note-Bartholomew-4)

## Functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Istana_Nurul_Iman&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Functions")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Istana_Nurul_Iman "Special:EditPage/Istana Nurul Iman") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(January 2013)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The Sultan takes his official audiences at the palace. The palace is also used for all the state functions of the government of Brunei. In addition, the palace houses the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister")'s office, and serves as the seat of the Brunei government.

In addition to audience and state rooms, there is a throne chamber used for various formal occasions such as the proclamation of the crown prince and the annual birthday investiture.

However, in keeping with its primary use as the Sultan's personal residence, the palace is the home to the Sultan's 7,000 cars in 110 separate garages. The cars are valued at around $5 billion.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Accessibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Istana_Nurul_Iman&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Accessibility")]

The palace is not open to the public except on the annual [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") celebration of [Eid-ul-Fitr](/wiki/Eid-ul-Fitr "Eid-ul-Fitr") (the festival at the end of the Muslim fasting month) when the palace receives about 110,000 visitors over a three-day period where the guests receive gifts of food as well as green packets containing money for young children. The palace is also open to Muslims during 10 days of the Ramadhan period for Tadarus and [Tarawih](/wiki/Tarawih "Tarawih") prayer gathering.
